data.title: In the past 24 hours, the entire network has liquidated $187 million, mainly long orders, and ETH liquidation exceeds BTC
data.description:
Odaily Planet Daily News Coinglass data shows that in the past 24 hours, the entire network has been liquidated for $187 million, and a total of 89,313 people have been liquidated; among them, long orders have been liquidated for $119 million, and short orders have been liquidated for $67.9046 million.
ETH liquidated $40.3045 million, exceeding BTC (liquidated $39.0865 million).

data.title: Sen. Warren urges Trump to back CFPB to fight debanking
data.description:
Odaily Planet Daily News Democratic Senator Elizabeth Warren urged Trump to work with regulators to stop the unwarranted closure of citizens and corporate bank accounts, and believed that attempts to suppress the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) would worsen the problem.
In a letter to Trump, Warren asked the new government to support the CFPB's efforts to prevent debanking. After firing Director Rohit Chopra, the Trump administration appointed Scott Bessent, a pro-cryptocurrency Treasury Secretary, as Acting Director. Bessent has stopped some activities of the bureau's staff.
Warren wrote before a Senate Banking Committee hearing on Wednesday that the issue involved both parties and emphasized that Trump recently publicly condemned Bank of America CEO Brian Moynihan. At the World Economic Forum in Davos, Trump accused the bank of restricting business with certain customers and accused JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon of doing the same to conservative customers.
“Sometimes banks have legitimate reasons and legal obligations to freeze or close bank accounts. But banks may implement these legal obligations in a sloppy and overbroad manner to cut costs,” Warren wrote in the Feb. 4 letter.
At a Senate committee hearing on Wednesday, Anchorage Digital CEO Nathan McCauley testified that he was a victim of debanking, but he declined to name the bank. McCauley said the bank told Anchorage in 2023 that it would close the company’s account because it was uncomfortable with Anchorage’s crypto customers.
“They refused to provide further explanation or allow us to speak with the risk management team, and we had great difficulty finding new banking partners,” McCauley said. “We spoke to approximately 40 banks across the country but were rejected, and many told us that they had a blanket policy prohibiting servicing crypto customers.”
A spokesperson for the Bank Policy Institute, which represents the banking industry, said in an emailed statement: “Banks must manage risks and meet regulators’ requirements, which may also limit banks’ disclosure of why accounts were closed. An important part of the solution is to fix the regulatory structure.” (Bloomberg)

data.title: Senator Warren Urges Trump To Address Banking Account Closures
data.description:
According to Odaily, Democratic Senator Elizabeth Warren has called on U.S. President Donald Trump to collaborate with regulatory bodies to prevent the unwarranted closure of bank accounts belonging to citizens and businesses. Warren criticized attempts to undermine the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), warning that such actions could exacerbate the issue. In a letter to Trump, Warren urged the administration to support the CFPB's efforts in combating the trend of 'de-banking.'

The Trump administration recently appointed Scott Bessent, a cryptocurrency advocate, as the acting director of the CFPB following the dismissal of Director Rohit Chopra. Bessent has reportedly instructed CFPB staff to halt certain activities.

Warren's letter, sent ahead of a Senate Banking Committee hearing, highlighted bipartisan concerns and noted Trump's recent public criticism of American Bank CEO Brian Moynihan. At the World Economic Forum in Davos, Trump accused the bank of limiting business with certain clients and similarly criticized JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon for allegedly discriminating against conservative customers.

In her February 4 letter, Warren acknowledged that banks sometimes have legitimate reasons and legal obligations to freeze or close accounts. However, she cautioned that banks might be executing these obligations in a hasty and overly broad manner to cut costs.

During the Senate committee hearing, Nathan McCauley, CEO of Anchorage Digital, testified about being a victim of de-banking. He refrained from naming the bank but stated that in 2023, the bank informed Anchorage of its decision to close the company's account due to concerns over Anchorage's cryptocurrency clients. McCauley expressed frustration over the lack of explanation and the difficulty in finding a new banking partner, having been rejected by approximately 40 banks nationwide. Many banks reportedly have blanket policies against servicing cryptocurrency clients.

A spokesperson for the Bank Policy Institute, representing the banking industry, stated in an email that banks must manage risks and comply with regulatory requirements, which may limit their ability to disclose reasons for account closures. The spokesperson emphasized that a key part of the solution is to reform the regulatory framework.

data.title: Phantom Wallet Achieves Record Monthly Revenue in January
data.description: According to BlockBeats, data from DeFiLlama reveals that Phantom Wallet's in-app exchange fees surpassed $29 million in January, marking a new monthly revenue record.

data.title: Coinbase Files Amicus Brief Urging Court to Protect Customer Assets in Prime Trust Bankruptcy
data.description:
Odaily Planet Daily News Coinbase has filed an amicus brief in Prime Trust's ongoing bankruptcy case, urging the court to support legal protections for customer assets held by custodians. Coinbase Chief Legal Officer Paul Grewal released the document, emphasizing that customer assets should not be included in the custodian's bankruptcy proceedings.
The company advocates for the enforcement of Article 8 of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC), which ensures that assets held by custodians belong to customers, not the custodians themselves. Coinbase argues that both its user agreement and Prime Trust's user agreement clearly stipulate that assets remain customer property. Bankruptcy should not change these agreements or reclassify assets as part of the custodian's property.
The legal document will prevent customer funds from being included in Prime Trust's bankruptcy property. Coinbase insists that maintaining Article 8 of the UCC is essential to maintaining trust in custodians, both in digital and traditional finance.
In other legal developments, the Operation Chokepoint 2.0 hearing has attracted attention from the entire industry. Paul Grewal will testify with other key figures to refute unfair banking restrictions on cryptocurrencies.

data.title: Researchers create s1 model comparable to OpenAI o1 model for less than $50
data.description:
According to a new research paper published last Friday, AI researchers at Stanford University and the University of Washington were able to train an AI "reasoning" model for less than $50 in cloud computing credits.
In tests that measure math and coding skills, the model, called s1, performed similarly to cutting-edge reasoning models such as OpenAI's o1 and DeepSeek's r1. The s1 model, as well as the data and code used to train it, are available on GitHub.
The team behind s1 said they created the AI ​​model through distillation, a process of extracting "reasoning" capabilities by training the answers of another AI model. Researchers said s1 was distilled from one of Google's reasoning models, Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking Experimental. Distillation is the same method used by Berkeley researchers to create an AI reasoning model for about $450 last month.
The researchers behind s1 are looking for the simplest way to achieve strong reasoning performance and "test time expansion," or to get AI models to think more before answering questions. These are some of the breakthroughs in OpenAI’s o1 that other AI labs have tried to replicate through various techniques. The s1 paper proposes that inference models can be refined using relatively small datasets using a process called supervised fine-tuning (SFT), in which an AI model is explicitly instructed to mimic certain behaviors in the dataset. SFT tends to be cheaper than the large-scale reinforcement learning approach DeepSeek used to train its answers to OpenAI’s o1, R1.

s1 is based on a small, off-the-shelf AI model from Alibaba’s Chinese AI lab Qwen, which is available for free download. To train s1, the researchers created a dataset of just 1,000 carefully curated questions, along with answers to those questions and the “thinking” process behind each answer from Google’s Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking Experimental.

After training s1 in less than 30 minutes using 16 Nvidia H100 GPUs, s1 achieved strong performance on some AI benchmarks, according to the researchers. Niklas Muennighoff, a Stanford researcher working on the project, says he can rent the necessary computers now for about $20. (TechCrunch)

data.title: New Mexico Senator Submits Bitcoin Strategic Reserve Bill, Proposing to Allocate 5% of Public Funds
data.description:
Odaily Planet Daily News U.S. Senator Ant Thornton submitted the "Strategic Bitcoin Reserve Act" to the New Mexico Senate, proposing to invest 5% of public funds in Bitcoin, aiming to establish an alternative treasury for the state. The proposed 5% allocation will diversify the state's funds and enable it to benefit from the long-term price appreciation of Bitcoin.
The bill also proposes that the Bitcoin reserve be managed by the state investment officer and operate under the supervision of the state investment committee. The bill also clarifies the need for cold storage as a viable means of protecting the state's Bitcoin.

data.title: New Mexico Senator Proposes Strategic Bitcoin Reserve Bill
data.description: According to Odaily, U.S. Senator Ant Thornton has introduced the 'Strategic Bitcoin Reserve Bill' to the New Mexico Senate. The proposal suggests investing 5% of public funds in Bitcoin to establish an alternative treasury for the state. This allocation aims to diversify the state's funds and capitalize on Bitcoin's potential long-term price appreciation. The bill also recommends that the state's investment officer manage the Bitcoin reserve under the oversight of the State Investment Council. Additionally, it emphasizes the importance of using cold storage as a viable method to safeguard the state's Bitcoin holdings.
